channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Nekome: Nazi Hunter's Title Really Says It All
item.content:encoded:
For some reason in recent years, I've seen the Dead Kennedys' song "Nazi Punks Fuck Off" referenced a lot. Maybe it's because I live in Seattle, or maybe it's something else. Regardless, when I saw that Nekome: Nazi Hunter would be at Summer Game Fest this year, I couldn't really ignore the title.

It's a fairly brutal melee action game where you punch, stab, and smash Nazis. The grindhouse cinema inspiration is really apparent, especially after I saw a prompt to "desecrate a corpse," which made the protagonist curb stomp its head. Needless to say, its depictions of violence aren't for the faint hearted.

The build I played was an early alpha, so there's still a lot that could change between now and the game's final release. While it had a few rough edges, there's some potential in Nekome: Nazi Hunter that would make it a solid title for some catharsis.

Revenge Is Best Served With Blood

The demo for Nekome: Nazi Hunter opens with a flair for the dramatic. Some Nazis are having a dinner, with a commanding officer giving them a classic Third Reich pep talk. It's hammy and pretty stereotypical; you've probably seen something similar from other media like Man in the High Castle.

Our protagonist, Vano Nastasu, infiltrates this dinner as the humble waiter. He wheels a cart to the commanding officer's side at the head of the table, revealing a severed Nazi head on his cart. Before the officer can really react, he gets a couple of stab wounds, and then the game gives you control of Vano.

The combat in Nekome: Nazi Hunter plays out similarly to the widely used Arkham system. One button attacks and snaps you to the nearest enemy in whatever direction you choose. Another button parries any incoming attack that's pretty clearly telegraphed over the enemy's head.

The simple system is serviceable, if not a little basic. The combat moves a little slowly, likely to create time and space for the gruesome canned finisher animations. They run a little long, but they show how vindictive Vano can be.

His thirst for revenge is revealed to us in a flashback, where we find his father figure dying in his shop from a Nazi attack. That soldier also stole his family's knife, and it was his dad's last request to get the precious heirloom back.

This led to the second half of the demo, where we chase after the soldier. He oh-so conveniently leads us to a Nazi compound full of mobs to kill. Some get progressively tougher, signified by their increased armor and more decorated uniforms.

It's a little easier to feel surrounded here, but there's a special ability you can activate after killing enough enemies that lets you tag up to three enemies and kill them in rapid succession. It does take quite a while to build up though, which can add to the monotony with the game's simple combat.

The saving grace in Nekome: Nazi Hunter is a small but wonderful touch that always put a smile on my face. There are lots of Nazi propaganda and flags all over the compound, and you can burn every single one. There's even a little side goal of burning at least five throughout the level. Is it a game-changer? No, but it's at least a nice little treat.

Nekome: Nazi Hunter is slated for an early 2027 release on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S, and if you're looking for some Nazi-slaying catharsis, you can wishlist it on Steam and support the developers.

Nekome: Nazi Hunter was previewed on a PC at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Robert Scarpinito is the Features Editor of TechRaptor. With a Bachelor of Arts in Journalism from the Ohio State University, sharing compelling stories is… More about Robert



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/nekome-nazi-hunters-title-really-says-it-all
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Bad Magpie Is Just As Chaotic And Delightful As You'd Hope It To Be
item.content:encoded:
At Xbox's Games Showcase 2026, indie developer Milktooth announced their debut game, Bad Magpie, where you take on the role of a magpie who is missing a wing, whose job it is to wreak absolute chaos on everything and everyone around them. As part of the show, I got my hands on with the game, and I can confirm it's JUST as chaotic and delightful as the trailer would have you believe.

Before getting our hands on the game were were introduced to it by Catherine Unger, Daisy Fernandez, and George Batchelor - Co-directors at Milktooth. As their debut title, Bad Magpie will invite players to "a dense world with exploration in any direction."

A unique aspect of the final game is that there's no text or dialogue; instead, the story moments that you witness or gameplay that you learn will all be based on your attentiveness to the game and willingness to explore and experiment.

The demo started out with our Magpie hopping down a forest trail. Immediately pressing random face buttons, I discovered how to peck, how to caw, and how to run. From here, the game began to slowly teach different object interactions in a way that a magpie might really enjoy, by putting shiny things around me.

I saw something interesting in a pot, so I learned how to break it. There was a shiny gem up a tree, so I pecked the trunk, and not only did the gem drop, but so did the wood. A gem on a rock taught me that by pecking rocks, I can start a spark, setting flammable things alight.

From there, I began to learn how to use things together. A large wooden plank was blocking my way, so I got a stick from a tree, put it near a rock, set it on fire, and carried it over to the wooden plank to open my way. This gameplay experience perfectly matched the idea of the emergent gameplay that Milktooth had advised they set out to create with Bad Magpie.

After making it through that more linear tutorial section of the game, the world expanded, first by teasing me with a trinket before a snake stole it, demanding 20 gems before it would return. Then by setting me loose in a nearby playground and buildings to try to gather them all up.

The puzzle variety here was a lot of fun. The simplest of these was to see a gem in a pot or trapped in a box, where pecking or setting something on fire was all that was necessary. Getting into a nearby building, there were gems that I would find by breaking into a TV, powering up a piano and placing items on certain keys, and setting boxes on fire.

The puzzle that I liked the most involved travelling to a soccer pitch where mice were kicking around a ball. I then popped the ball and walked it through the goal, scoring me a point and flinging cheese off the scoreboard. Using that cheese, I baited the mice over to a chalk outline of the planet with mice holding hands around it. After five mice entered the circle, a gem appeared.

There was no indication on the circle that that's what you're meant to do, but by bringing a different mouse into it I saw the chalk change. This experimentation, testing, and then figuring out all of the pieces to solve the puzzle was incredibly satisfying and will bring a lot of smiles if the rest of the game is like this.

I think anyone would assume as much from the trailer, but it's good to be able to confirm that this game is just as "Untitled Goose Game" as you would hope it to be.

Concluding the demo, I saw a brief cutscene having collected the trinket and providing it to the star. Who the star is, what purpose they have on early, and why shiny trinkets might help them are all still unknown quantities.

Bad Magpie is coming out in 2027 for Xbox Series X|S, Xbox on PC, and Xbox Cloud. It will also be an Xbox Game Pass title and Xbox Play Anywhere.

Bad Magpie was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2027

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/bad-magpie-is-just-as-chaotic-and-delightful-as-youd-hope-it-to-be
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Join Us Scared Me, But I'm Now Ready To Convert
item.content:encoded:
There was a lot happening at Summer Game Fest, so when Join Us was revealed at the Xbox Showcase, I was unfortunately in the middle of appointments. So when I got a chance to sit down and play Join Us, I knew absolutely nothing other than it was a game about a cult and it had very intimidating artwork.

Stepping into the meeting room, I was greeted with flickering candles, Polaroid photos with scribbling on them, and the wonderful co-founders of Wolf Haus Games: Michael Abbot, Joe Dietsch, and Pierre-Olivier Chartrand.

"Join Us is about building a doomsday cult to survive the end of the world"

Before we sat down with the game, we got a brief overview of the game. In Join Us you play the role of a cult member who has been promoted by the glorious Leader to run a smaller offshoot of his cult. You can play this game single-player or can play with up to three of your friends in 4-player co-op.

Dialing it back slightly, those of us in the room were clued into the history of development company Wolf Haus Games. Starting as a solo development project in 2021, it wasn't long before the company began to grow. At the moment, Wolf Haus Games is a group of 10 developers based in Montreal.

The demo that we got to play of Join Us covered the beginning of the game and some early combat scenarios. The full title will feature a deeper section of resource management and building gameplay, where the player will recruit new followers who might be interested in following the cause, grow crops to make sure that your cult will remain sustainable, and develop key buildings such as your church, living spaces, and other structures of worship.

Loading up Join Us I got to create my cult and cultist. Picking robes, colors, and symbols, we had an identity. From there, I picked one of three characters with a loadout to take the role of.

I still didn't quite know what the vibe of Join Us was meant to be. At this point, nothing had given me more of an indication of what it was until I loaded into the world and stepped outside and took in the commune for the first time. I saw cultists welcoming each other with loving phrases, some happily gardening, and others doing exercise stretches and tai chi, and it twigged to me that Join Us is not a serious game.

A tutorial on shooting and navigation, later I was being welcomed to the Leaders church to hear about my new role to move to… Bedford County to become the head of a sect of the cult in my own right.

"I hope one day I get to be a business person" - A cultist as I was being welcomed to the Leaders church

Even through the tutorial section, the humor was on point. Picking up a gun for the first time in front of a pen of chickens, I was directed by the Leader to try shooting the targets. I killed the chickens so quickly to progress through the demo, I didn't even notice the circle of pumpkin-headed scarecrow dummies before the Leader chimed in to say that he was proud of me for how eager I was to kill on his command… but that killing the chickens was not necessary…

Once I had arrived at my new settlement, it was already set up for me and full of cultists to follow my command. This is the part of the game that players will have to do themselves in the full game, spending resources to build new structures and make sure that their commune is well equipped to house, feed, and make happy new followers of their faith.

The portion of the game that I did get to see was when a local militia showed up to try to cull my followers. At any time, the nearby groups who aren't a fan of the work that your cult is doing might show up. It's then your job not only to shut them down, but hunt them back to their point of origin and make sure they can't be more of an issue.

For where the game currently is in development, the basic gameplay is all present; that being said, you are a very squishy individual. There were melee options for combat, but with how quickly I was getting thrown around during a gunfight, you're likely going to find yourself hanging back as far as you can while trying to take a few precise shots as you approach an enemy militia base.

An interesting aspect of Join Us is that even while you convert new cultists and set up satellite locations, is that you're not really playing as the cultist but are channeling your energies and purpose through them. Because of that, at any point while playing, you can bring up a map and select any of your cultists and immediately begin acting from their perspective instead.

Speaking with the co-founders, I was interested to hear what exactly would happen if you lost all of your followers. I was informed that in the worst-case scenario, where you lose absolutely all of your followers, and you haven't had a chance to recruit more, the Leader will show you some pity and send more to you.

In my extra moments, I was also shown the ability tree and was clued into a few of the ways that your cult could develop as the game goes on. Different paths have you becoming different types of cults, if you lean into a more healthy-earth/druidic style of cult you might get to the point where your cult doesn't eat meat. That might mean that you need to over-produce different types of food, but the plus side is that wild animals are not only going to be friendly to your cult but they'll join you on attacks.

Other skill tree paths I saw included getting better at propaganda, steering into the occult so even a dead cultist might show up as a ghost and help till your fields or fight for your cause, and from the trailer, we've also seen tentacles in the sky that can point down a Lovecraftian path.

I went into Join Us knowing absolutely nothing and being afraid that it would be an incredibly serious title about building up a Cult. The experience I got was far from it. Join Us manages to combine city-building gameplay with a very unserious take on cult creation.

There's a fun and dark twist to what might otherwise be a very normal town-building game with combat/shooting elements, but the premise that has been landed on to create a town of strange cultists is a fantastic one.

Join Us was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/join-us-scared-me-but-im-now-ready-to-convert
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Saw: Genesis Is An Asymmetrical Horror Game Of Cat And Mouse That Doesn't Quite Seem To Match The Canon
item.content:encoded:
Saw: Genesis was revealed at Summer Games Fest Showcase 2026. This 3v1 asymmetrical horror title is the first step into multiplayer for Bloober Team. Set in 1920, you’ll play as either the Judge or the Accused as you seek to push your victims towards redemption or work together to escape from the ever-present threat.

At Summer Game Fest, we got to sit down and learn more behind closed doors (and right next to a dismembered body).

Bloober Team, known for a variety of horror titles including Layers of Fear and Silent Hill 2, is going to be taking its first step into the realms of multiplayer with Saw: Genesis. While this is new, it’s all part of Bloober Team’s “journey to become the master of horror in the video game space.”

Taking place in London 1920, after the First World War, Saw: Genesis will somewhat continue the themes of the modern-day series that the Jigsaw character, in this game called the Judge, seeks to reprimand and try to ‘redeem’ the accused as a consequence for something they did in conjunction with the war.

The game is set 100 years before the present day because “it gives [Bloober Team] a little bit of creative freedom. Lionsgate is very particular about their brand, which makes sense. Giving that little space gives us a bit of artistic freedom for how we approach our version of the Jigsaw, which is much more brutal and raw.”

As we heard the voice of Jigsaw in the trailer, I was interested to learn if there was going to be any story moments in the game where the plot of Saw: Genesis would tie in to the present-day setting, or how we would learn more about the characters' circumstances that led them to being captured by the Judge.

“First of all, this is being developed as a long-lasting live service game, so you never know, right? But for right now, there are no plans for that. Everything we currently have planned, different Judges, biomes, etc., takes place 100 years ago.”

For information specific to the story behind individual customers, there are no plans to highlight those characters within the game itself. While there might be plans in the future to release themed trailers or promotional material related to the different Accused, for now, there isn’t anything like banter between the characters.

A comparison was made that if Jigsaw in the present is the perfected form of the killer, then the Judge character in Saw: Genesis is the unrefined version, too early yet.

To get a better idea of what the gameplay loop of Saw: Genesis would consist of, we were shown some cut-together gameplay of a round, including the perspectives of the Judge and the Accused. It’s expected that games of Saw: Genesis will take somewhere between 10 and 15 minutes to complete.

The Accused Must Do What They Can To Survive

As a member of the Accused, you find yourself waking up in a procedurally generated labyrinth of rooms. You’ll need to explore and obtain key items that can be used to unlock a final puzzle room before you can escape.

The setup was likened to the premise of Saw 2.

Leaning into the idea of Saw-like puzzles, you won’t just be opening chests and lockers to obtain items, but there will also be locked, trapped containers that might have a powerful item in them, where you need to damage your character in order to obtain the reward.

In the gameplay we were shown, we saw a metal lever that needed to be flipped that had been heated to the point where it was glowing orange. The player scalded their hand as they slowly flipped the lever, revealing a metal pipe within.

While it wasn’t expanded upon too much in the gameplay, we were shown that each of the Accused will have a variety of perks at their disposal that are tied to the theme of their character.

The Judge Of Saw: Genesis Stalks And Sets A Trap

“You’re the mastermind, you’re in control, you want to trap the Accused in Redemption traps and make them repent.”

As the Judge, you will watch the Accused from a ‘Judge-only’ location, like walking through the rafters that you can see and affect the experience of the players, but you’re safe from any kind of danger.

It’s your job to set traps and isolate the players from one another, then, when you’ve got them trapped, you’ll be able to pick them up and take them to a Redemption trap. As the Accused, if you’re put into one of these traps, you’ll either have to be freed by a fellow Accused, or you might “sacrifice one of your limbs. If you do, it will affect your gameplay depending on if you lose an eye, a hand, or a leg.”

Interestingly, it was brought up early and quite candidly that they’re aware this is a tough genre to try to create a game for. Titles like Dead By Daylight and Outlast Trials were mentioned, but then the rest is a “sea of dead games.”

“We cannot be a reskin of Dead By Daylight, so our take on this genre is that, first and foremost, to be true to the Saw franchise, our killer is vulnerable. It’s not a powerful monster that can be killed and always gets back up.”

The Judge not being a powerful force will mean players will have to do more work to get themselves in advantageous situations. “When you get jumped by the Accused, if you get killed, you basically lose.”

Saw: Genesis Will Be A Premium Title, Upcoming Closed Alpha

Saw: Genesis will be releasing on PC via Steam Early Access, the current expected timeline will be a Q4 2026 release in Early Access though that could be pushed back depending on the feedback from upcoming Closed Alpha tests and public demos.

The game will likely be coming to additional platforms, but for now the focus is on ensuring the experience is solid from a gameplay perspective, and then more features in the form of Judges and Accused will be added.

Coming off the heels of the announcement, Saw: Genesis already has a variety of plans for continued exposure and a roadmap to release. “We’re going for a Closed Alpha in June, and we already started taking on subscribers. We’re going to have plenty of players to choose from to do a proper test, but we’re not announcing exactly when that’s going to take place.”

The game will be a premium title, so it will cost to purchase the game in Early Access and at launch. While there wasn’t anything specifically to reveal regarding monetization, “there are certainly discussions about other monetization schemes, like season passes or cosmetics.” One thing that Bloober Team is certain of this early though, is that “there is going to be no pay-to-win mechanics, as it goes against everything we believe.”

Early Days, But It Seems Like Saw In Name Alone

Saw: Genesis is a big swing for Bloober Team to break into the multiplayer space, and while what they have gameplay-wise seems like it tackles asymmetrical horror gameplay quite well its hard to shake the feeling that it doesn’t really feel like it totally fits the world and themes of Saw.

Some early logic issues include why the Judge would capture the Accused to let them loose, and THEN try to get them into a rehabilitation trap instead of just placing them in one. For a series all about learning about failures, facing them, and pushing past human limits to come out redeemed on the other side the lack of any depth in character stories, or mechanics to do with confronting their history, feels like there’s an acknowledgment of this aspect of Saw but no steps taken to implement it other than the awareness that it’s taking place.

It very much feels like a strong 3v1 horror game about a cunning mastermind stalking prey for fun and torturing them that has had a heavy coat of paint applied to it to make what mechanics might have already been in development to look like a Saw game.

It’s still early days for Saw: Genesis, so there’s plenty of time for more to be revealed that might see the gameplay elements align with what a lot of Saw franchise fans will see as Saw.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/saw-genesis-is-asymmetrical-horror-game-of-cat-and-mouse-that-doesnt-quite-seem-to
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Minecraft Dungeons II Is Rebuilding The Structure While Adding So Much More - Interview
item.content:encoded:
Xbox at Summer Game Fest had an explosive showing from large AAA announcements like Halo Campaign Evolved to indie games about being a mischievous little magpie. Among those was the reveal of Minecraft Dungeons II, a sequel to the Diablo-like Minecraft adventure released in 2021.

Last weekend, we were invited to hear from Mans Olson, Creative Director for Minecraft Dungeons II, and Laura De Llorens, Design Director for Minecraft Dungeons II, about creating a new Minecraft Dungeons title and the major changes that fans of the original will see.

"We had all of these ideas for what to put in the game, and we had a bunch of suggestions from the community," Olson began, "when we took all of that and really thought about it, it didn't really make sense to keep adding to the first video game, we felt it was better for us to make a sequel so we could fully realize those ideas."

Getting into the specifics, there are three different ways that Minecraft Dungeons II is going to be drastically different from the first game.

How Minecraft Dungeons II Iterates On The Formula

The first major change is in exploration. In the first Minecraft Dungeons, there were linear missions that you would select from a hub, but in the sequel, it's a larger interconnected world that you can mostly walk across from the start until the end of the game.

The way that Minecraft Dungeons II treats dungeons is as levels and loading zones to cross and continue your journey and exploration.

Olson was very clear that "we are purposefully not calling it an open world, it's more of a guided experience than a traditional open world game. But it has a lot more freedom to explore, and it's a really cool world."

Detailing more of what could be expected of the overworld exploration, Olson added that players will see new biomes and new environments, some of which have never been seen in Minecraft before.

"The world is very fun to explore, and we really tried to make sure that there are a lot of things for you to discover in nooks and crannies."

For those who tend to get lost when left to their own devices on a large map, never fear. At any point, you can also summon a Guiding Light; this will create a trail from the player to their next objective. You can play through Minecraft Dungeons II by wandering around and discovering everything along the way, or beelining it to your next major objective.

Multiplayer For Everyone, In Every Configuration

The second upgrade is in the enhancements to multiplayer, and wanting to make sure that everyone can play from wherever they are. Olson detailed that Minecraft Dungeons II is up to four players with coop, but also explained that couch co-op and online multiplayer could be freely intermingled. Three people on the couch with one player online, or two on the couch and two online, etc.

"We have friend lists, we have matchmaking, we have party codes, we have offline play, and we have crossplay. Basically, any wya that you can imagine wanting to play this game together you can."

When playing Minecraft Dungeons II myself, there were a number of other quality-of-life improvements to the multiplayer that make playing the game with a large (and possibly chaotic) group a better experience. At any time, you can bring up your inventory as a smaller menu at the bottom of the screen; this doesn't pause gameplay for anyone else, and the menu is quick and easy to navigate, allowing for fast changes.

Additionally, while exploring, I had found a secret chest with loot as I split off from the rest of my party. When asked how to get to where I was, De Llorens showed us that at any time, one player could teleport to the location of another. This meant that I didn't need to try to explain the path I took, or worry about anyone having issues with the platforming; everyone could just enjoy the benefits. This will be a great benefit for parties where some members are young or not as experienced with games.

The third upgrade is in the combat and movement. Continuing to lean into the flashiness and arcade quality of the original game, you'll still see big combos and powerful attacks. On top of that, there are new movement abilities and new abilities that let you engage with your enemies in a different way.

Olson picked up a controller to demonstrate that you could now jump in Minecraft Dungeons II, and with that, you can also do jump attacks. The sword that was equipped to the player character we were seeing also had an additional ability that, when used in a jump attack, would send out a fiery shockwave in a cone from the player.

Another change to combat and the character progression is that there's no longer just one slot for equipment, but four. Mimicking the armor setup of classic Minecraft, you can now equip a helmet, torso armor, pants, and boots. This armor can also now drop in the "Unique" rarity.

Loot For Everyone

A nice touch for loot in Minecraft Dungeons II is that whenever you open a chest, there will be a drop for every player, that can only be picked up by the player. This is represented in the game by the item having a colored glow around it that correlates to the player.

I did ask De Llorens what options were in place for color blind players to better identify when equipment was intended for them. De Llorens informed me that there are a variety of color blind settings in the game that let you swap the player colors to anything to allow them to stand out. Additionally, the items will occasionally show an indicator over the item for who is meant to pick it up. This is a hovering icon that just says "P3" for player 3.

Minecraft Dungeons II will be available on Xbox Series X|S, Xbox on PC, Xbox Cloud, Steam, PlayStation 5, Nintendo Switch, and Nintendo Switch 2. It will also be available day one on Xbox Game Pass and will be an Xbox Play Anywhere title.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/minecraft-dungeons-ii-is-rebuilding-structure-while-adding-so-much-more-interview
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Prepare To Face Your Fears In Alien Isolation 2
item.content:encoded:
One of the tensest games I’ve played in my life is Alien Isolation. The ever-present fear of an Alien or Synth stalking you as you attempt to stealthily make your way through a spaceship left my shoulders by my ears, and heart rate high. So, of course, given the opportunity to play an hour of Alien Isolation 2 at Summer Game Fest, I leapt at the chance to get scared once again.

In Alien Isolation 2, you play as Blake (I don’t think I ever caught her first name), a new addition to a research team on a distant planet with a Wayland-Yutani outpost. An interesting aspect about this planet is that it’s constantly being ravaged by powerful storms that routinely cut off contact and the ability to move around the colony.

The demo picked up as Blake was talking to Cole and Otto, two men who were already present on the colony. After a bright light flashes through the sky, you set out from your armored car to investigate the source of the light. Tension is brought among the party due to the oncoming storm, but on Blake's orders, you find the source of light… a crashed spaceship.

Alien Isolation 2 Sets A New Bar In Environmental Effects

Exploring the outside world to locate this crashed ship, you make your way through a dense wooded area and crouch to fit through mud tunnels when the fallen trees make progress difficult.

The sound of the rain and thunder, matched with the incredible lighting work whenever lightning would illuminate the sky, was absolutely phenomenal. Enjoying the ambiance of the overworld section of the game (and being somewhat scared to progress towards the guaranteed horror and death) I wanted to stay here for far longer, but pushed ahead.

If you're more interested in Alien games that focus on action instead of horror you can check out our preview of Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2

Instead of waiting until after the storm to investigate further, Blake makes the dumb horror movie choice to instead hop into the derelict spaceship to see what caused it to fall and to check for any survivors.

Descending down a ladder, the noise of the storm mostly faded away, and I was left with eerie quiet and the sound of my footsteps on metal, that was until a mostly broken Working Joe collapsed towards me before shutting off.

From the clues of the environment, we learned that the Working Joe was one of the Seegson models. We also learned that the ship that had collapsed is actually the hazmat lab of the Sevastopol Station, the location of the events of the first Alien: Isolation title. This might indicate that the planet that Alien: Isolation 2 takes place on is KG-348, but at this time, that’s speculation.

After learning this little tidbit, Blake freezes in place as a fully grown Xenomorph appears from the ceiling, beginning the ‘hunt’.

For the rest of the demo, my goal was to make it out of the spaceship without the Alien locating me, and that was TOUGH.

There were two ways to make it back to the ladder where I came from. I could go back the way that I came, which required the navigation through a number of hallways, a medical bay, and more corridors before reaching the ladder.

The alternate path was that I had found a large wheel that let me shut off the gas that was fueling a fire blocking a more direct route to the ladder. The trade-off here was the amount of distance that I would need to travel vs the time I would be a sitting duck out in the open, turning the valve so that the Alien could come up behind me, grab me, and kill me.

Knowing that there were two ways to get out was easy enough to work with on paper, but I died a LOT. I got to witness the Alien grabbing me out in the open, crawling through a vent that I was also hiding in to surprise me, and in one attempt where I made a sprint for the ladder, I was shocked when Blake stopped and looked down, revealing she had been punctured through by the tail of the Xenomorph.

We were informed that as the game continues, you’ll get access to a variety of weapons and other tools, but for this demo, all we had available to us were two flares. These are beneficial when thrown to distract the Xenomorph for a bit, but they also make a big commotion when you strike them up in your hand, making the precise timing of when to use them and when to just hide a bit difficult, but done right, it can really save you.

Improvement To Spatial Audio Will Make Alient Isolation 2 Even Better

One aspect of the game that did seem like it needs to be further refined is the spatial audio system. It’s very clear while wearing headphones that the audio is set up in a way to allow you to pick up on the heavy footfalls of the Alien. That being said, the range that the noises of the footfalls would sound like they’re right on top of you, even when you can see the Xenomorph a room away from you and behind glass, did make it difficult to know when to hunker in place and when to make a break for it.

Hiding in a vent in the floor with footfalls that sounded like the Alien was right on top of me, I spent an extended period of time waiting in place for the Xenomorph to physically appear before me so that I could actually trust that I understood where it was in relation to me. The alternative was to make a guess, charge forward, and that normally ended up with the Xenomorph and I both in the same tiny corridor before the “Game Over” screen would flash in my face, forcing me to reset from my last save.

While the demo ended almost as soon as I made it to the ladder, I’m sad to report we do not leave Blake in a safe position. Getting most of the way up the ladder, the Alien grabbed her and was ready to kill her before the stormwater pushed the Hazmat Bay off a cliff, leaving the fate of Blake and her new best friend, the Xenomorph up in the air.

Standing up from the darkness of the demo bay, I did a quick check, and my heart rate was certainly up, and my hands were trembling. With a bit of fine-tuning done on the audio system to give the player more of a fighting chance, I think Alien Isolation fans are going to be very happy with the sequel.

Having played the original game back on the Xbox One with my Kinect hooked up, I was a fan of the settings where the Alien could hear audio from the room of those playing the game. Another fun feature was to use the Kinect tracking to peek around corners. I asked the team running the demo if there would be any hope of those features being brought forward and was told that there wasn’t anything in place for the moment, but that they were also fans of the audio features so who knows.

Alien Isolation 2 was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026
